title: Extracting Universal Entanglement Scaling from Mixed Fermionic Gaussian States via Entanglement Projected Entropy
abstract: Identifying spatial quantum correlations in mixed states is challenging because thermal mixed-state contributions obscure the entanglement encoded in subsystem entropy. Here, we introduce the entanglement projected entropy, a diagnostic for extracting boundary-sensitive quantum correlations from mixed fermionic Gaussian states. By resolving subsystem entropy into Gaussian entanglement channels and projecting their purification partners onto the physical complement, we obtain a closed-form expression in terms of the physical covariance matrix. In a one-dimensional free-fermion chain, it removes the volume-law mixed-state background and recovers the zero-temperature conformal scaling with the $c/3$ coefficient. In a two-dimensional half-filled $π$-flux model, it reveals a universal finite-temperature scaling collapse governed by an effective Dirac infrared length. These results establish entanglement projected entropy as a Gaussian spatial filter for boundary-sensitive quantum correlations hidden in mixed-state entropy.
summary: The paper introduces a new diagnostic called Entanglement Projected Entropy (EPE) designed to extract quantum correlations from mixed states. By filtering out the volume-law thermal background, EPE successfully recovers universal entanglement scaling in both 1D and 2D fermionic models. This allows for the identification of quantum signatures that are otherwise hidden by thermal fluctuations.
main_problem: Identifying spatial quantum correlations in mixed states is difficult because thermal/classical contributions to subsystem entropy scale with volume, obscuring boundary-sensitive entanglement signatures.
main_result: The authors introduce Entanglement Projected Entropy (EPE), a diagnostic that filters out extensive thermal backgrounds to recover universal entanglement scaling, such as the c/3 coefficient in 1D and universal scaling collapse in 2D.
method: The method involves embedding mixed fermionic Gaussian states into a globally pure state via an ancilla environment and using a directional projection weight to isolate entanglement channels residing in the physical complement.
model_or_system: The study focuses on mixed fermionic Gaussian states, specifically a 1D critical free-fermion chain and a 2D half-filled pi-flux model on a square lattice.
key_observables: Entanglement Projected Entropy (EPE), von Neumann entropy, Mutual Information, and the directional weight (w_q).
important_parameters: Inverse temperature (beta), central charge (c), UV cutoff (a), and the effective Dirac infrared length (L_Dirac).
main_assumptions: The derivations and EPE formulation are specifically tailored to and limited to Gaussian fermionic states.
figures_summary: Figure 1(a) provides a schematic of the purification process where a mixed state is embedded into a larger pure state with an ancilla environment.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the EPE diagnostic, develops its mathematical framework for Gaussian states, provides a closed-form expression using the covariance matrix, and then applies it to 1D and 2D models to demonstrate its ability to recover universal scaling and topological signatures.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and open quantum systems as it provides a new tool to distinguish true quantum entanglement from thermal noise in mixed-state many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Purification picture underlying the entanglement pro- jected entropy. A mixed state ρS on the physical bipartition S = A⊕B is embedded into a pure state |ΨS E⟩on the enlarged Hilbert space A ⊕B ⊕E, such that ρS = TrE |ΨS E⟩⟨ΨS E|. (b),(c) Schematic illus- tration of the spatial filtering mechanism in Gaussian states. Blue circles denote physical fermionic modes in A and B, while orange circles denote ancilla modes in E. The curve drawn over subsys- tem A shows the spatial wavefunction amplitude of a single-particle entanglement mode ⃗χq on the sites in A, while the curve over B ⊕E shows the amplitude distribution of its unique purification partner ⃗ηq. The...

FIG. 2. Numerical verification of the universal CFT scaling in an infinite 1D free-fermion chain at finite temperature. (a) Projected entropy EB(A) (left axis, solid lines) and standard von Neumann en- tropy S(A) (right axis, red dashed line) as a function of the subsystem size ℓ. While S(A) exhibits an extensive thermal volume law (linear in ℓ), EB(A) cleanly removes this thermal background and saturates to a plateau for ℓ≫vFβ. (b) Data collapse of EB(A) against the ef- fective conformal length ln(Leff), with Leff defined in Eq. (11). Data from various inverse temperatures β collapse onto a single universal straight line. The dashed gray line indicates the analytical CFT pre- diction with...

FIG. 3. (a) Half-filled π-flux model on a square lattice. Blue and red bonds denote hopping amplitudes −t and t, respectively, yielding a π flux per plaquette. The dashed line with length Ly marks the bipar- tition into A and B, with A a vertical strip of width ℓx. (b) Scaling of the bipartite EPE (subtracted by a constant) [ϵNcut −EB(A)] /Ncut versus 1/LDirac for different inverse temperatures β, with Ncut = 2Ly and LDirac defined in Eq. (16). The dashed line serves as a guide to the eye for the linear scaling form.

FIG. 4. Projected entropy in the SSH chain for a half-chain biparti- tion. (a) Temperature dependence of EB(A) for several values of t2/t1 in the topological regime. The dashed line marks ln 2. (b) Projected entropy EB(A) as a function of t2/t1 for several fixed temperatures. For comparison, half of the mutual information, I/2, at T = 0.2 is also shown as a dashed line, demonstrating its close agreement with EB(A) at T = 0.2.
